objective: Write a complete, specific A/B test plan for a Zomato checkout change — covering hypothesis, variants, primary metric, guardrail metrics, minimum detectable effect, and estimated duration with reasoning.

realWorldAnchor.example: Swiggy reportedly runs hundreds of concurrent A/B experiments at any given time across checkout, restaurant ranking, and delivery ETA display — before any checkout flow change ships to all users, it runs as a controlled experiment on a percentage of traffic, with guardrail metrics monitoring for unintended side effects like increased order cancellations or payment failures.

teamContext: A/B testing is the PM's primary tool for working with the Data team to validate a decision before committing to it — the PM writes the hypothesis and specifies the metrics, the analyst sets up the instrumentation and calculates sample size, and both review the results together. A PM who cannot specify guardrail metrics forces the analyst to guess what 'winning' means.

tasks.title: A/B Testing: A Complete Guide for Product Managers — Product School
tasks.summary: This article covers A/B testing from a PM's perspective — how to design a valid experiment, what makes a hypothesis testable, how to choose a primary metric vs guardrail metrics, and why statistical significance alone is not enough to declare a winner. For Pranav, this is the practical reference for writing the A/B test plan in today's assessment.
keyTakeaways: A valid A/B test hypothesis has three parts: the change you are making (the treatment), the metric you expect to move (the primary metric), and the direction and magnitude of the expected change — 'removing the tip prompt will increase checkout completion rate by at least 5%' is a hypothesis; 'removing the tip prompt will improve UX' is not

Guardrail metrics are the metrics you are NOT trying to move but must monitor to ensure the experiment does not cause harm — for a checkout test, guardrail metrics typically include average order value, payment failure rate, and order cancellation rate — if any guardrail moves negatively, the experiment fails even if the primary metric improves

Sample size and duration are calculated before the experiment starts, not after — the minimum detectable effect (the smallest change you would consider business-significant) drives the sample size calculation, and underpowered experiments produce results you cannot trust either way

tasks.title: Experimentation at Scale: How to Build a Culture of Testing — First Round Review
tasks.summary: This First Round Review article covers how product teams build an experimentation culture — not just how to run individual A/B tests, but how to decide which experiments are worth running, how to handle inconclusive results without reverting to opinions, and how to communicate experiment results to stakeholders who want a cleaner answer than 'it depends.' For Pranav, this adds the organisational dimension to the technical framework from the first reading.
keyTakeaways: The biggest waste in A/B testing is running experiments without a clear hypothesis — teams that test everything without prioritising end up with a backlog of inconclusive results and no learning, because they tested features rather than specific mechanisms

Inconclusive results are not failures — they are information: if a well-powered experiment shows no difference, you now know the change does not move the needle, which is itself valuable for deprioritising that direction and focusing elsewhere

Communicating experiment results to non-data stakeholders (design leads, engineering managers, business heads) requires translating statistical significance into business terms: 'at 95% confidence, the new checkout reduces friction for 1.2 million users per month, generating an estimated 40,000 additional completed orders per month' is what a PM should bring to a decision meeting, not a p-value

tasks.keyword: A/B testing product manager checkout flow India Swiggy Zomato experiment design case study 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing how Indian consumer internet companies design and communicate A/B experiments — specifically for checkout flows where conversion rate improvements translate directly to revenue — gives Pranav real anchoring for writing his own test plan in the assessment. Look for PM blog posts, conference talks, or product teardowns that describe a specific experiment, its hypothesis, and what the team learned.
whatToLearn: What primary metrics Indian e-commerce and food delivery PMs use for checkout experiments — checkout completion rate, time-to-complete, or order value are common choices, each with different implications for sample size and duration

How Indian product teams handle A/B test duration given India's day-of-week and festive season traffic patterns — running an experiment that includes a Sunday (high traffic) and misses a Monday (lower traffic) produces a biased result, so test duration decisions in India require knowledge of the product's weekly traffic curve

tasks.promptTitle: Design and stress-test an A/B test plan — Zomato tip prompt removal
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice designing a rigorous A/B test plan for a real product change.

Today I studied:
- A/B test hypothesis structure: change + primary metric + expected direction and magnitude
- Guardrail metrics: metrics you must monitor to ensure the experiment does not cause unintended harm
- Minimum detectable effect and sample size: calculated before the experiment starts, not after
- How to communicate experiment results to non-data stakeholders in business terms

Real world context: I am an APM at Zomato. The current checkout flow has 5 steps: (1) Review cart, (2) Select delivery address, (3) Tip prompt screen (users can add a tip for the delivery partner — default is 0), (4) Select payment method, (5) Confirm and pay. The hypothesis is that the tip prompt screen adds unnecessary friction and removing it will increase checkout completion.

Your role today: Act as a skeptical data scientist reviewing my test plan before I submit it to the engineering team for implementation. Push back on any hypothesis that is not testable, any primary metric that is too broad, any missing guardrail, and any duration estimate that is not justified.

Activity:
Step 1: I write the hypothesis in the required format: 'By [change], we expect [primary metric] to [direction] by [minimum magnitude] because [mechanism].' You critique the hypothesis.
Step 2: I name the primary metric and 3 guardrail metrics. You push back on any that are missing or poorly chosen.
Step 3: I estimate the minimum detectable effect (MDE) and explain why I chose that magnitude. You challenge whether it is realistic and business-significant.
Step 4: I estimate the test duration and explain how I got there. You check whether it accounts for India-specific traffic patterns.

After all 4 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — the tip prompt for delivery partners has a specific emotional and ethical dimension in India that affects how users respond to removing it: delivery partner welfare is a visible public concern in Indian consumer discourse, and a change that removes the tipping option may create negative PR even if it improves conversion — this is a risk factor that a US-market PM might not weight]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav on A/B test design]

Begin with: 'Let's start with the hypothesis. The change you are testing is: removing the tip prompt screen from Zomato checkout. Write the hypothesis in the exact format I specified. Go.'

bonusTasks.title: Statistical significance vs practical significance — PM perspective
bonusTasks.keyword: statistical significance practical significance A/B test product manager explained simply examples 2022 2023
bonusTasks.summary: One of the most common A/B testing mistakes is declaring a winner based on statistical significance alone without asking whether the change is practically significant — i.e., large enough to justify the engineering cost and maintenance overhead. Searching for PM-specific explanations of this distinction builds the intuition Pranav needs to pressure-test experiment results rather than accept them at face value.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write a complete A/B test plan for removing the tip prompt step from Zomato's checkout flow — covering hypothesis, control variant, treatment variant, primary metric, 3 guardrail metrics, minimum detectable effect with justification, and estimated test duration with reasoning.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at Zomato. Current checkout flow: (1) Review cart → (2) Select delivery address → (3) Tip prompt — users see a screen asking if they want to add a tip for the delivery partner; the default is 0, options are ₹10, ₹20, ₹30, or custom → (4) Select payment method → (5) Confirm and pay.

Data you have:
- Overall checkout completion rate (users who reach Step 1 and complete Step 5): 58%
- Drop-off specifically at the tip prompt screen (Step 3): 12% of users who reach Step 3 do not continue
- Average orders per day on the platform: 1.8 million
- 40% of users choose 'tip ₹0' (i.e., they go through the screen and add nothing)
- Average delivery partner tip per completed order: ₹4.20
- No legal or contractual requirement to show the tip prompt

Write a complete A/B test plan with these sections:
1. HYPOTHESIS — in the format: 'By [change], we expect [primary metric] to [direction] by at least [minimum magnitude] because [mechanism].'
2. CONTROL VARIANT — describe the experience
3. TREATMENT VARIANT — describe the experience
4. PRIMARY METRIC — name it, define it, explain why it is the right primary metric
5. GUARDRAIL METRICS — list 3, each with a 1-sentence explanation of what it is watching for
6. MINIMUM DETECTABLE EFFECT (MDE) — state the smallest improvement you would consider business-significant, and justify why you chose that number
7. ESTIMATED DURATION — how many days will you run the test? Show your reasoning (consider traffic volume, weekly patterns, and minimum sample size).
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Seven clearly labelled sections (Hypothesis / Control / Treatment / Primary Metric / Guardrail Metrics / MDE / Duration). Each section: 2-5 sentences or a short structured list. The Hypothesis and Duration sections must include explicit reasoning, not just a number.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 10: A/B Testing and Experimentation.

I studied today:
- A/B test hypothesis structure: change + primary metric + expected direction and magnitude + mechanism
- Guardrail metrics: metrics monitored to detect unintended harm from the experiment
- Minimum detectable effect: the smallest change that would be business-significant, calculated before the experiment starts
- How to communicate experiment results to stakeholders in business terms, not statistical terms

Real world context I should apply:
Zomato checkout — removing the tip prompt screen. Current checkout completion: 58%. Drop-off at tip prompt: 12% of users who reach it. 1.8 million daily orders. Average delivery partner tip: ₹4.20 per completed order.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Write a complete 7-section A/B test plan for this experiment.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his full A/B test plan here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — especially check: is the hypothesis testable and specific? Are the guardrail metrics genuinely measuring harm or just replicating the primary metric? Is the MDE justified or arbitrary? Does the duration account for Zomato's weekly traffic pattern?]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., the ethical and PR dimension of removing a delivery partner tip option; the impact on average order value if users who currently tip ₹10-30 stop tipping entirely; whether the experiment should segment by user type (first-time vs repeat) since tip prompt friction affects these groups differently]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about the delivery partner tipping context in India — unlike the US where tipping is expected and the amount is large, Indian food delivery tipping is voluntary and small, but delivery partner welfare is a visible and emotionally charged topic in Indian consumer discourse — removing the tip option entirely vs keeping it opt-in has a different public perception risk than in other markets]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Fluency with Data — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
